_comment: SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for all 5 landing-page versions. Facts/copy live here once; each bespoke page draws from this. Update numbers HERE only.

product.name: Evolve Loop
product.oneLiner: Any agent can write code overnight. Evolve Loop is the layer that decides whether it's safe to merge — adversarially, structurally, with memory that compounds.
product.oneBreath: Your AI wrote the code. A different AI tried to break it. A bash exit code — not an opinion — decided whether it ships.

hero.headline: Ship the code your agent wrote — without reading the diff.
hero.headlineLead: Ship the code your agent wrote —
headlineVariants: Ship the code your agent wrote — without reading the diff.
red_count == 0 → ship. Everything else is an opinion.
Merge while you sleep. The lessons survive the crash.
An auditor that can't be fooled. A ledger that can't be forged.
hero.subhead: Every AI change runs an adversarial pipeline: a rival model tries to break it, exit codes — not opinions — gate the merge, and each failure becomes a lesson the next cycle reads.

problem.heading: Agents can write the feature now. The hard part is merging it.
body: Agents can write a feature, fix a bug, or refactor a module — unattended. The question isn't can it write the code? anymore. It's can you merge it without re-reading every diff?

The usual answer — "ask another LLM if it looks done" — is the weakest link. A model grading code, often a sibling of the one that wrote it, shares its blind spots and rewards confident prose over correctness.

theTurn.heading: LLMs do the judgment. Deterministic code owns the verdict.
body: Scout finds the work, Builder writes it, Auditor hunts for what's wrong — judgment only a model does well. Everything deterministic — phase order, write scoping, the ship gate, the verdict, the ledger — lives in the Go kernel, where the model gets no vote.
A model can be wrong, biased, even adversarial. It still can't reorder phases, write outside its worktree, ship without a green verdict, or forge the ledger. Those aren't prompts — they're code.

phaseSpine.blurb: Turns a vague goal into 8 structured fields + at least one challenged premise.
Finds the real work, cites research, reads prior lessons before choosing.
Bounds the cycle — top_n / deferred / dropped — so scope can't blob.
Writes the code + predicates inside an isolated git worktree.
A different model family cross-checks; the predicate suite runs; the verdict is binary PASS/FAIL.
Commits to main only if red_count == 0. Tamper-evident, SHA-chained.
Every failure becomes a durable lesson the next cycle's Scout reads.

pillars.title: Verdicts are bash exit codes, not LLM prose
pillars.claim: The ship gate reads a number, not a vibe.
pillars.proof: Predicates are executable scripts; their exit code is the verdict. The model can write prose all day — only acs-verdict.json:red_count == 0 ships. You can't hallucinate a passing test.

pillars.title: Three layers of structural anti-gaming
pillars.claim: Hard to fool, because control lives in code, not judgment.
pillars.proof: Tier 1: PreToolUse hooks + a SHA-chained ledger — tamper one past entry and every later link breaks. Tier 2: OS sandbox + a per-cycle worktree. Tier 3: adversarial defaults. Tier 1 is non-negotiable.

pillars.title: Adversarial by construction
pillars.claim: The reviewer is built to refute, not rubber-stamp.
pillars.proof: The Auditor is a different model family from the Builder (Opus auditing Sonnet), prompted to break the work. A separate TDD engineer writes the failing tests before any code ships — author and verifier are never the same model.

pillars.title: Memory that compounds across runs
pillars.claim: The loop gets smarter so you don't repeat yourself.
pillars.proof: Failures become YAML lessons merged into state. 61 of them now fire across 367 cycles — some written 90 cycles ago still catch bugs. Reflexion, wired to evidence-bound storage.

pillars.title: Vendor-agnostic, per-phase routing
pillars.claim: The best model for each role. No lock-in.
pillars.proof: A Go resolver routes each phase independently — Scout, Builder, and Auditor can each run a different model. Claude, Gemini, and Codex adapters ship; the orchestrator doesn't care which ran.

incident.heading: The framework caught its own bugs.
incident.body: A Gemini-3.1-pro experiment shipped 7 bugs — a Builder that skipped Scout's deliverable, an Auditor citing out-of-scope files, a mis-set flag. None reached you silently: the predicate suite, ledger forensics, and retrospective caught all seven, and within 48 hours each became a permanent guard. The point isn't "cycles never fail" — it's "failures become fixes."

comparison.heading: When to reach for it.
rows: Different model family, prompted to refute
comparison.note: Devin, OpenHands, and Aider are agents. Evolve Loop is the governance layer that drives them and adds verification + memory. We don't race the agent — we decide whether you can merge what it wrote, unread.

quickstart.heading: Your first cycle is one command away.

finalCta.heading: Stop reading diffs you didn't write.
finalCta.subhead: Open source. MIT. Runs on Claude Code, Gemini CLI, and Codex CLI.

footer.grounding: Grounded in Reflexion (Shinn et al. 2023) and double-loop learning (Argyris & Schön 1978).
